title: Going Home Checklist [pdf]

en.description: A demo PDF resource. Tests the in-app PDF viewer with a sample discharge checklist.

content.markdown:
## Getting ready to go home

Discharge planning starts early — the care team will begin working with you on a discharge plan as soon as your child is stable.

Use this checklist to track what's been covered, what questions you still have, and what you need to arrange before leaving the hospital.

notes: DEMO ONLY — for testing the PDF viewer. Remove before production.

title: Visitor Guidelines [pdf]

en.description: A demo PDF resource. Tests the in-app PDF viewer with sample visitor guidelines.

content.markdown:
## Who can visit, and when

The PICU has specific visiting policies to protect all patients, including your child. Before visitors come, it's worth reading through the guidelines below.

Key points:
- All visitors must wash hands or use hand sanitiser on entry
- Children under 12 may have different access rules depending on sibling status
- The number of visitors in the room at one time may be limited

notes: DEMO ONLY — for testing the PDF viewer. Remove before production.

title: Hand Hygiene in the Hospital

en.description: A demo YouTube resource. Tests the in-app video player. Replace this URL with a real MGB/WHO video before going live.

content.markdown:
## Why hand hygiene matters

One of the most important ways to protect your child in the PICU is clean hands. Every person who enters the room — staff, visitors, and caregivers — should wash their hands or use hand sanitiser before touching your child or any equipment.

Watch this short video to see the recommended technique:

## What the PICU team does

The care team follows strict hand hygiene protocols at every patient contact. Here is a second video showing the full clinical technique used in hospital settings:

es.description: Un recurso de demostración de YouTube. Pone a prueba el reproductor de video integrado en la aplicación. Reemplace esta URL por un video real de MGB/W

content.markdown:
## Por qué es importante la higiene de manos

Una de las formas más importantes de proteger a su hijo en la UCIP es mantener las manos limpias. Toda persona que entre en la habitación —personal, visitantes y cuidadores— debe lavarse las manos o utilizar desinfectante de manos antes de tocar a su hijo o cualquier equipo.

Vea este breve video para conocer la técnica recomendada:

title: Guided Breathing Exercise]

en.description: A demo YouTube resource. Tests the in-app video player with a short breathing exercise. Replace with a real video before going live.

content.markdown:
## A moment for yourself

It's easy to forget to breathe when everything feels heavy. This short exercise takes about 2 minutes — less time than it takes to wait for the elevator.

### Make it a habit

Even 2–3 minutes of focused breathing can lower your heart rate and help you think more clearly. Try it once or twice a day — before rounds, after a hard conversation, or at the end of the day.

notes: DEMO ONLY — swap sourceUrl for a real MGB mindfulness video. Remove before production.

title: Hand Hygiene — Text, Video & PDF

en.description: A demo resource testing all three content block types in one screen: a Markdown intro, a YouTube video, and a tappable PDF card.

content.markdown:
## Why hand hygiene matters

One of the most effective ways to protect your child from infection in the PICU is clean hands — for everyone who enters the room.

Watch the short video below, then tap the card to download the quick-reference guide.

## When to wash

- **Before** touching your child or any equipment
- **After** using the bathroom
- **After** touching your face, phone, or any surface outside the room
- **After** removing gloves or a gown

Alcohol hand gel is on the wall at every room entrance — use it every time.

notes: DEMO ONLY — tests all three ContentBlock types (text, video, pdf) in a single resource. Remove before production.

title: Welcome to the PICU

en.description: An introduction to the Pediatric Intensive Care Unit — what to expect, how the unit works, and how to find your way around.

content.markdown:
## Welcome

Being here is hard. The unit can feel overwhelming at first — the sounds of monitors, the rhythms of care rounds, new faces every shift. You're not expected to absorb it all at once.

### The first day

- **Your nurse** is your main point of contact each shift
- **Care team rounds** happen every morning — you're welcome to be there and ask questions
- **Social work** can help with housing, meals, time off from work, and emotional support

Everyone on the team is here for your family. Please ask — no question is too small.

notes: Pull from existing PICU welcome packet / admission materials.

title: Meet Your Care Team

en.description: Who's who in the PICU — nurses, intensivists, fellows, residents, respiratory therapists, and support staff.

content.markdown:
## Attending Physician

This is the doctor who leads the medical team that plans the care and treatment for your child.

They will work closely with you to make decisions about your child's care, and are ultimately responsible for the medical care provided by the team.

Your child's Attending Physician will either be:

- A Pediatrician who specializes in Hospital Medicine called a "Hospitalist"
- A Subspecialist Pediatrician, who has a specialty in a certain area of pediatrics (such as a Pediatric Pulmonologist who specializes in children's lung problems)
- A physician from your child's primary care practice

If you are not sure who your child's Attending Physician is, please ask us.

## Consulting Physician

This is a Pediatrician who has a specialty in a specific area and is called in to "consult" on your child's case. They will work with the medical team to diagnose, treat, and care for your child. They may also work with a **Pediatric Fellow**, who is a doctor that has completed medical residency training in Pediatrics and is now in training to become a subspecialist (e.g., Pediatric Pulmonologist). The Consulting Physician and Fellow will make recommendations to the Attending Physician and resident team.

## Resident Physician or House Officer

This is a doctor who has graduated from medical school and is now in a resident training program to become a Pediatrician. A Pediatric resident program usually takes 3 years to complete.

- **Interns** (first year residents) will examine your child every day and are usually the first person called if you have a question or concern. Your child will have a primary Intern during their stay.
- **Second year residents** are in between their first and third year.
- **Senior Residents** (third year) oversee the work of medical interns and medical students.

Residents work with the Attending Physician to give your child the best care possible. There is a Resident Physician available 24 hours a day.

## Medical Students

Medical students are in the third and fourth year of medical school. Third year students work with Interns to help care for your child. Fourth year students usually work under the Senior Resident.

All members of your child's medical team should introduce themselves and their role. **If you are uncertain who a person is, please ask them to introduce themselves.**

notes: Include role descriptions. Avoid stereotyped gender depictions.

title: The PICU Daily Routine

en.description: What a typical day looks like in the PICU — rounds, shift changes, and the best times to ask questions.

content.markdown:
## The PICU Daily Routine

What a typical day looks like in the PICU — rounds, shift changes, and the best times to ask questions.

Here's what to know as you get oriented to the unit and how things work.

### Getting settled

- **Ask your nurse** for a unit tour when you arrive
- **Meet the team** during morning rounds — they'll introduce themselves
- **Family resource room** has snacks, a coffee machine, and quiet space
- **Charge stations** are at the bedside and in the family lounge

notes: Ask nursing leadership for current rounds schedule and shift change times.

title: Visiting Hours & Policies

en.description: Who can visit, when, and what to expect — including sibling and extended family policies.

content.markdown:
## Visiting guidelines

Two designated caregivers may be at the bedside at any time. Additional family and friends are welcome during open visiting hours.

### Hours

- **Open visiting:** 8am – 10pm daily
- **Overnight:** One caregiver may stay overnight at the bedside
- **Siblings:** Welcome during open hours — child life can help prepare them for the visit

During care team rounds (typically 8–11am), please remain at the bedside if you want to participate, or step out briefly if the team needs private discussion.

### Health guidelines

- If you have **fever, cough, or GI symptoms**, please don't visit until 24 hours symptom-free
- **Hand hygiene** is required on entering and leaving the room
- **Flu shots** are strongly recommended for all visitors during flu season

notes: Confirm current visiting policy with Patient Relations.

title: Wi-Fi & Staying Connected

en.description: How to connect to the hospital Wi-Fi network and stay in touch with family and friends.

content.markdown:
## Wi-Fi

Free guest Wi-Fi is available throughout the hospital.

- **Network:** `Guest-Wifi`
- **Password:** No password required; accept terms on the splash page
- **Speed:** Sufficient for video calls and streaming

If you have trouble connecting, check with your unit clerk or call the help desk at extension 5000.

notes: Get current network name and login process from IT or front desk.

title: Interpreter Services

en.description: Free interpreter services are available 24/7 for caregivers who prefer to speak in another language.

content.markdown:
## Language access

Every family has the right to communicate with their care team in their preferred language. Interpretation is available 24/7, at no cost to you.

### How to request an interpreter

- **For scheduled meetings:** Ask your nurse at least an hour ahead if possible
- **For urgent needs:** Interpreters are available by phone or video within minutes
- **Written materials:** Available in Spanish, Portuguese, Mandarin, Cantonese, Haitian Creole, Arabic, and several other languages

If a family member wants to interpret, please still ask for a professional interpreter — medical decisions deserve the clearest possible communication.

notes: Confirm languages supported and how to request an interpreter.

title: What to Bring

en.description: A checklist of essentials to pack for yourself and your child when preparing for a PICU admission.

content.markdown:
## Packing for your stay

Some planned items help your child feel more at home — but try not to overpack. The room has limited space.

### Bring

- 2–3 comfortable outfits
- A favorite blanket or stuffed animal
- Slippers or grippy socks
- Phone charger (long cord!)
- A water bottle
- Books, tablet, or small games
- Any comfort item from home

### Leave at home

- Valuables, cash beyond $20
- Fresh flowers (not allowed in ICU rooms)
- Electric candles or anything with an open cord

The hospital provides toiletries, but feel free to bring your own if you prefer.

notes: Create a practical packing list — clothing, toiletries, chargers, insurance cards, comfort items.

en.description: What intensive care means, why your child may need it, and what day-to-day care looks like in the PICU.

content.markdown:
## The PICU environment

The pediatric ICU provides the highest level of care for children with serious illnesses. The pace, the equipment, and the staffing ratios are all designed for close observation and rapid response.

### What you'll see

- **Monitors** displaying heart rate, breathing, oxygen, and blood pressure — alarms are normal and don't always mean something is wrong
- **IV poles** with medication pumps — these automatically deliver precise doses
- **Ventilator** if your child needs breathing support
- **Feeding tubes, catheters, or drains** depending on what care requires

### Staffing

PICU nurses care for only 1–2 patients at a time, which means someone is nearly always watching. Doctors are on the unit 24 hours. Respiratory therapists, pharmacists, and other specialists are available around the clock.

### It's a lot, and that's normal

Most families describe the first days as emotionally exhausting. Your care team knows that — ask about the Family Resource Room, chaplaincy, social work, or any other support that might help.

content.caption: A short breathing exercise for moments when things feel like too much.

title: Lines, Tubes & Monitors

en.description: Plain-language explanations of the tubes, lines, and monitors you'll see connected to your child.

content.markdown:
## IV lines, tubes, and monitors

You'll see a lot of tubes and wires connected to your child. Here's what the main ones do.

### Common lines

- **Peripheral IV:** A small tube in a vein for giving fluids or medicine
- **Central line:** A longer IV placed in a larger vein — used when medicines can't go through a small IV
- **Arterial line:** Monitors blood pressure continuously and allows blood draws without new sticks

### Breathing support

- **Nasal cannula:** Small prongs delivering a little extra oxygen
- **High-flow:** Warmed, humidified oxygen at higher rates
- **BiPAP/CPAP:** A snug mask that helps pressure the airways open
- **Ventilator (breathing tube):** A tube that breathes for the child — used when the child can't breathe enough on their own
**Ask your nurse at any time** which lines and equipment your child has and what each one does. It's normal for this to change from shift to shift as the plan evolves.

notes: Avoid alarming language. Normalise the equipment without downplaying how stressful it is.

title: Understanding the Monitors

en.description: What the numbers on the bedside monitor mean — heart rate, oxygen saturation, blood pressure, and more.

content.markdown:
## What the monitor shows

The bedside monitor shows four main numbers continuously.

### The numbers

- **HR (heart rate):** Green, usually 60–160 depending on age
- **RR (respiratory rate):** White or yellow, 15–40 for most kids
- **SpO2 (oxygen saturation):** Blue, 95–100% normally (lower targets for some conditions)
- **BP (blood pressure):** Red or purple, either continuous or every 15 minutes

### About alarms

Alarms sound often — most are brief variations that correct themselves within seconds. Don't panic. Your nurse will check, silence the alarm if it's a false reading, or respond if it matters.

If a sustained alarm is making your child uncomfortable or you're worried, ask the nurse — that's what they're there for.

notes: Include a note that alarms don't always mean something is wrong — normalise the sounds.

title: Breathing Support & the Ventilator

en.description: How the ventilator works and what breathing support means for your child's care.

content.markdown:
## What a ventilator does

A ventilator breathes for a child whose lungs need support. The breathing tube (ETT) goes through the mouth or nose and into the windpipe.

### What you'll see and hear

- Rhythmic mechanical breathing sounds
- The number of breaths per minute and pressures on a screen
- Suction carried out periodically to clear secretions

### The weaning process

As your child improves, the ventilator gradually does less work. Eventually they breathe more on their own until the tube can come out. This process varies from hours to weeks depending on the situation.

Ask the respiratory therapist at any shift how things are going — they'll explain what the current settings mean.

notes: Written for caregivers with no medical background. Use simple analogies.

en.description: How the team manages your child's pain and discomfort, and how you can help comfort your child.

content.markdown:
## Keeping your child comfortable

Pain is assessed every shift using age-appropriate tools. You know your child best — please speak up if something seems off.

### How comfort is managed

- **Scheduled medications** for expected pain
- **As-needed doses** for breakthrough pain
- **Non-medication comfort:** Music, swaddling, dim lights, your presence
- **Child life:** Distraction techniques, procedural support, sibling support

### If you're worried

Pain in kids can look like fussiness, facial grimacing, or unusual stillness. Trust your read — if something doesn't look right, ask your nurse.

title: Medications in the PICU

en.description: General information about how medications are given in the PICU and how to ask questions about your child's treatment.

content.markdown:
## Medications in the PICU

General information about how medications are given in the PICU and how to ask questions about your child's treatment.

Understanding what's happening clinically can make hard moments less scary. Here's a guide.

### Ask your care team

- Write down questions as they come up — rounds can move quickly
- Ask for diagrams or printed material if a concept would help
- It's fine to ask the same question twice on different days
- Your bedside nurse can explain things at the pace that works for you

notes: Do not list specific drugs — too variable. Focus on how to ask and what to write down.

en.description: How your child may receive nutrition in the PICU — tube feeds, IV nutrition, and when they can eat or drink.

content.markdown:
## Nutrition & Feeding in the PICU

How your child may receive nutrition in the PICU — tube feeds, IV nutrition, and when they can eat or drink.

content.markdown:
## Taking Care of Yourself

Practical reminders about eating, sleeping, and taking breaks — because you matter too.

Caregiving takes a toll. Here are resources to help you take care of yourself, too.

### Small things that help

- Step outside for 10 minutes, even when it feels like you shouldn't
- Eat something, even if you're not hungry
- Keep one routine from home — coffee, a podcast, a phone call
- Accept help when people offer

notes: Tone must be warm and validating — not preachy. Acknowledge how hard this is.

en.description: Retail pharmacy locations and hours at MGH for filling prescriptions before discharge.

content.markdown:
## MGH retail pharmacy

The MGH Outpatient Pharmacy is located on the ground floor of the Wang Ambulatory Care Center. Discharge prescriptions can be filled here before you leave.

### Hours and contact

- **Monday–Friday:** 7am – 8pm
- **Saturday:** 9am – 5pm
- **Sunday:** Closed

Ask your nurse to call ahead so prescriptions are ready at discharge.

en.description: Retail pharmacy location and hours at NWH for discharge prescriptions.

content.markdown:
## NWH retail pharmacy

Newton-Wellesley's main retail pharmacy is on the first floor near the Washington Street entrance, just past the main lobby.

### Hours and contact

- **Monday–Friday:** 8am – 6pm
- **Saturday:** 9am – 2pm
- **Sunday:** Closed

After hours, CVS on Washington Street (a 5-minute drive) is 24-hour.

en.description: Retail pharmacy locations and hours at BWH including Longwood campus options.

content.markdown:
## BWH retail pharmacy

The BWH Outpatient Pharmacy is in the 75 Francis Street lobby, next to the main information desk. A second location is inside the Shapiro Building.

### Hours and contact

- **75 Francis Street:** Mon–Fri 7:30am – 7pm, Sat 9am – 3pm
- **Shapiro Building:** Mon–Fri 8am – 6pm

Longwood CVS (Brookline Ave) is open late for after-hours needs.

en.description: Getting around the MGH main campus — buildings, entrances, and key landmarks.

content.markdown:
## MGH main campus

MGH spans several connected buildings around Blossom Street. The main entrance is at **55 Fruit Street** (White Building).

### Key buildings

- **White Building:** Main entrance, information desk, chapel
- **Ellison Building:** Adult inpatient units
- **Lunder Building:** Cancer center, imaging
- **Wang Ambulatory Care Center:** Clinics, pharmacy

The PICU is in the Ellison Building, 17th floor.

en.description: Getting around Newton-Wellesley Hospital — entrances, parking, and unit locations.

content.markdown:
## Newton-Wellesley campus

NWH has a single connected campus at **2014 Washington Street**. The main entrance is off Washington Street via the front drive.

### Key areas

- **Main entrance:** Information desk, cafeteria
- **North Building:** Surgery, recovery
- **South Building:** NICU, maternity
- **Allen Riddle Building:** Clinics, outpatient services

Valet parking is available weekdays at the main entrance.

en.description: Navigating the BWH Longwood campus — entrances, connectors, and building names.

content.markdown:
## BWH Longwood campus

BWH spans multiple connected buildings in the Longwood Medical Area. The primary address is **75 Francis Street**.

### Key buildings

- **75 Francis Street (L1):** Main entrance, information, chapel
- **Shapiro Cardiovascular Center:** Cardiac, pharmacy
- **Connors Center for Women's Health:** OB/GYN, maternal-fetal
- **Tower Building:** Inpatient adult units

The Pike Bridge connects Brigham to Children's Hospital for families with children at both.

en.description: Parking garages, rates, and validation options at the MGH main campus.

content.markdown:
## MGH parking

The **Fruit Street Garage** is the most convenient option for families visiting inpatient units, including the PICU.

### Rates and validation

- **Standard rate:** $20 flat daily, $36 maximum
- **Family validation:** Ask the unit clerk — discounted weekly passes are available for inpatient families
- **Overnight parking:** Permitted; use the same ticket on exit

Accept cash or credit at the exit kiosks.

en.description: Parking options and rates at Newton-Wellesley Hospital.

content.markdown:
## NWH parking

NWH has free patient and visitor parking in two on-campus lots. Valet parking is available weekdays 7am – 5pm for a small fee.

### Lot options

- **North Lot:** Closest to main entrance
- **South Lot:** Closest to NICU / maternity
- **Valet:** Main entrance, $8/day

No validation needed — parking is complimentary for patients and visitors.

en.description: Garage options, rates, and discount passes for the BWH Longwood campus.

content.markdown:
## BWH parking

The **75 Francis Street Garage** is the primary visitor garage. The **Fenwood Road Garage** is a shorter walk for the Connors Center.

### Rates and validation

- **Standard:** $22 up to 4 hours, $36 daily max
- **Family discount card:** Available at the Information Desk — $12 flat for families of inpatients
- **Weekly pass:** $90, purchased at the Patient & Family Relations office

Evening rates (after 5pm) are reduced.

en.description: Coffee shops and grab-and-go cafés at MGH for a quick break between visits.

content.markdown:
## MGH cafés

When the cafeteria feels like too much, a quiet café can be a welcome break.

### Options on campus

- **Starbucks (Main Lobby):** 5:30am – 9pm daily
- **Au Bon Pain (Wang Lobby):** Mon–Fri 6am – 8pm
- **Blue State Coffee (Yawkey):** Mon–Fri 7am – 5pm
- **Cambridge Street Coffee Cart:** Mon–Fri 7am – 2pm

Most accept Apple Pay and badge swipe for staff.

en.description: Cafés, coffee stations, and nearby options at Newton-Wellesley.

content.markdown:
## Newton-Wellesley cafés

NWH has a smaller footprint than the Boston campuses, but there are still options for a coffee break.

### Options

- **NWH Café (Main Building):** 6:30am – 7pm daily — self-serve coffee, pastries
- **Tatte Bakery (across the street):** 7am – 6pm, full breakfast and lunch
- **Starbucks (West Newton):** 5 minutes by car

The main café also has grab-and-go sandwiches after cafeteria hours.

en.description: Coffee shops and cafés across the BWH Longwood campus.

content.markdown:
## BWH cafés

Longwood has no shortage of caffeine options, both on the BWH campus and in the surrounding medical area.

### On campus

- **Au Bon Pain (75 Francis Lobby):** 5:30am – 9pm
- **Shapiro Café:** Mon–Fri 6am – 7pm
- **Peet's Coffee (Brookline Ave):** 6am – 7pm

### Nearby

- **Dunkin' (Longwood Ave):** 24 hours
- **Tatte Bakery (Brookline Ave):** 7am – 8pm

en.description: Getting to MGH by T, bus, and commuter rail.

content.markdown:
## Getting to MGH by T

**Red Line — Charles/MGH stop** is the closest T station, directly across the street from the main entrance.

### Transit options

- **Red Line:** Charles/MGH stop (walking distance)
- **Green Line:** Transfer at Park Street
- **Commuter Rail:** North Station (5-minute walk)
- **Bus:** Routes 43, 55, CT2 stop nearby

MassHealth transportation (PT-1) is available for eligible families — ask social work.

en.description: Transit options to reach Newton-Wellesley Hospital.

content.markdown:
## Getting to NWH by transit

NWH is best reached by car, but several transit options exist for families without a vehicle.

### Transit options

- **Commuter Rail (Framingham/Worcester Line):** Newton Highlands or Auburndale stop, then bus or rideshare
- **MBTA Bus:** Routes 553, 554, 556 stop on Washington Street
- **Green Line D:** Newton Highlands, then a 15-minute bus ride

There is no direct T stop. Plan extra travel time.

en.description: Getting to BWH Longwood by T, bus, and Longwood Medical Area shuttle.

content.markdown:
## Getting to BWH by T

BWH is in the Longwood Medical Area. **Green Line E — Longwood Medical Area stop** drops you at the doorstep of 75 Francis Street.

### Transit options

- **Green Line E:** Longwood Medical Area stop
- **Bus:** Routes 8, 19, 47, 60, 65, 66 all serve Longwood
- **LMA Shuttle:** Free inter-campus shuttle connects BWH, BCH, Beth Israel, Dana-Farber
- **Commuter Rail:** Ruggles station, then shuttle or 15-minute walk

The LMA Shuttle runs every 10 minutes during weekdays.

en.description: Getting started with pumping — schedules, storage, and support from the lactation team.

content.markdown:
## Getting started with pumping

If you're planning to provide breastmilk for your baby, starting to pump within the first few hours after delivery helps establish supply. Our lactation team can visit bedside to help you get started.

### Pumping schedule

- Aim for **8–10 pumping sessions per 24 hours** in the early days
- Sessions should be around 15–20 minutes each
- Night pumping matters — prolactin levels are highest at night

Ask your nurse to page the lactation consultant if you'd like one-on-one support.

### Storing milk on the unit

Every NICU has a dedicated milk fridge and freezer. Labels and storage containers are at the bedside — your nurse will walk you through the labeling process.

en.description: How pasteurized donor breastmilk is used in the NICU when mother's own milk isn't available yet.

content.markdown:
## About donor breastmilk

If your own milk supply isn't yet established, donor milk is often used as a bridge. It's pasteurized, screened, and sourced from an accredited milk bank.

### How the consent works

The care team will ask for your written consent before using donor milk. You can ask questions about screening, processing, and how it's stored at any time.

**Note:** Donor milk is used only while your own supply is being established — it's not a long-term alternative.

en.description: The benefits and practical steps of skin-to-skin contact with your NICU baby.

content.markdown:
## Why skin-to-skin matters

Skin-to-skin contact — often called kangaroo care — helps regulate your baby's heart rate, temperature, and breathing. It also supports breastfeeding and bonding.

### How it works

1. Let your nurse know you'd like to hold
2. Wear a button-down shirt or open-front top
3. Your nurse helps transfer baby carefully, monitoring equipment
4. Aim for 60+ minute holds when possible — shorter holds are less regulating

Both parents can participate. Research shows benefits for fathers and non-birthing partners too.

content.caption: A gentle breathing exercise to help you relax during skin-to-skin.

en.description: Understanding engagement and stress signals in preterm and medically complex infants.

content.markdown:
## Your baby communicates from day one

Even very preterm babies send signals about what they need. Learning to read these cues helps you respond in ways that feel calming and safe for your baby.

### Engagement cues (baby is ready for interaction)

- Relaxed hands and face
- Eye gaze, even briefly
- Steady breathing
- Reaching or grasping

### Stress cues (baby needs a break)

- Splayed fingers, arched back
- Turning head away, averting gaze
- Hiccups or sneezes
- Color changes

Your bedside nurse and the developmental care team can help you practice reading these signals.
